text: Data storytelling
Correlation
Causation
Confounding variable
Statistical significance
Outlier
Margin of error
Leading indicator
Actionable insight
Sample size
Once we control for tenure, the gap narrows but does not close, and it is that residual gap I am reporting.
Drop the outlier and the effect shrinks but survives. Left in, it skews the whole story toward the flattering explanation.
The fastest way to lose a research audience is to read too much into a chart that shows correlation and call it proof.
After all the honesty, I can still tease out the one signal that survives every rival explanation.
My job is to connect the dots into a single actionable insight, not to make each number say more than it can.
It is estimated that turnover among new managers rose by twelve percent last year.
Turnover is thought to be closely linked to how onboarding is run.
The drop is believed to reflect the reorganization, though it cannot be proven.
Retention is reported to have improved since the mentorship program began.
Your first slide has two lines moving together. Before you say a word, tell me: are you about to claim the program caused that?

No. It is a correlation, and I will not read causation into it. The reorganization landed in the same quarter. That is my confounding variable, and I could not fully control for it. So I am reporting a link, not a cause.
Good. And this team here, the one that tripled? I assume that is where your recommendation comes from.

It is not. That team is an outlier, and it skews the mean on a sample size this small. Set it aside and the effect shrinks but survives. It is the version that survives that I am asking you to act on, not the flattering one.
So after all the caveats, what is left? Give me one thing this council can actually do.

One thing. New joiners are twice as likely as tenured staff to leave, and that gap holds after I control for tenure. So treat the onboarding window as a leading indicator and watch it monthly. That is my actionable insight, and it does not depend on the causal claim I refused to make.
Engagement is thought to be linked to onboarding, but I would not yet call that causation.
New joiners are twice as likely as tenured staff to leave in the first year.
The reorganization is the confounding variable I could not control for, so I am flagging it myself.
What survives every rival explanation is a leading indicator, and that is the one thing I am asking you to act on.

Danielle, it is Claire, the night before the council. I have seen your deck, and the finding is a good one, so let me protect it from the one mistake that will sink it in that room. You have a slide that shows engagement rising and turnover falling in the same three months, and it is tempting to stand up and say the mentorship program did that. Do not say it. The moment you turn a correlation into a cause in front of Alan Prescott, he stops listening to the rest of you, and he is right to. Here is what I have watched work. Name the confounding variable yourself, out loud, before he can. The reorganization landed in exactly those three months too, and either of them could be moving the numbers, so say so. Then do the thing that is genuinely yours, which is to tease out the one signal that survives both explanations, and to call it what it is, a leading indicator, not a proof. And keep the margin of error on the slide. I know it makes the number look smaller. It also makes you look like someone who has done this before. The people who overclaim in that room are never invited back to it.

Claire, it is Alan. You asked what would make me trust a set of HR numbers, so here it is, and you may pass it to her. I am not difficult about data. I am difficult about people who read too much into it. If she shows me two lines moving together and tells me one caused the other, we are finished in the first minute, because that is not a finding, it is a coincidence with ambition. What I am actually listening for is whether she knows what she cannot say. If she tells me the sample is small and shows me the margin of error without being asked, I relax. If she names the variable she could not control for before I raise it, I lean in. And if, after all of that honesty, she can still tease out one signal and tell me plainly what a manager should do with it on Monday, then she has done something most consultants never do in a year, which is to turn a number into a decision without lying about what the number is. That is the whole job. The rest is decoration.

New joiners are twice as likely as tenured staff to leave in the first year.
The drop is believed to reflect the reorganization, though it cannot be proven.
I could not fully control for the reorganization, so I am reporting the residual effect, not the raw one.
Engagement is thought to be linked to onboarding, but I would not yet call that causation.
The reorganization is the confounding variable I could not control for, so I am flagging it myself.
What survives every rival explanation is a leading indicator, and that is the one thing I am asking you to act on.
